data.title: As U.S. Stocks Hit Records, Do Mexican Equities Offer Diversification Opportunities?
data.description:
By Erik Norland

At a Glance

  • Mexican equities offer potential diversification opportunities from U.S. stocks because of their historically low correlation
  • When looking at total returns for each index, it’s important to consider currency performance

As U.S. stocks continue to hit record highs, some investors might be looking beyond its borders for portfolio diversification. Mexico, with its distinct economic landscape and newly introduced futures contract on the Mexican S&P BMV IPC index, may present an opportunity. It's a timely question to ask: Are Mexican stocks a bargain compared to their U.S. counterparts?

Valuation and Industry Differences

When examining price-to-earnings ratios, Mexican stocks appear to be significantly cheaper compared to their U.S. counterparts. The S&P 500 is trading at around 25 times its estimated 2025 earnings, whereas the Mexican stock market is valued at just 13 times earnings.

However, this valuation gap is complicated by macroeconomic factors. Mexican interest rates are currently double those in the U.S., and this higher rate environment translates into a higher discount factor applied to Mexican earnings, making the relative value proposition on a price-to-earnings basis somewhat ambiguous. But there's a lot more to know beyond valuation levels about Mexican equities.

Comparing Performance: Local vs. Same Currency

Looking back to the year 2000, Mexican equities have shown strong performance in local currency terms. The S&P/BMV IPC Index has delivered returns roughly twice those of the Russell 2000, Nasdaq 100 and S&P 500.

However, the Mexican peso has depreciated by about 50% against the U.S. dollar over the same period. When viewed in U.S. dollar terms, Mexico's stock market has returned almost the same as the major U.S. indices since 2000.

What's particularly noteworthy is the timing of these returns. From 2000 to 2012, Mexican stocks were the standout performers. Since 2012, U.S. stocks have taken the lead. This suggests that Mexican equities and U.S. stocks have delivered different returns at different times, making the Mexican market a potential portfolio diversifier.

The Case for Portfolio Diversification

The average daily correlation between Mexican and U.S. stocks over the past dozen years has averaged around 0.4, indicating a relatively weak correlation.

The sector composition of the Mexican stock market also differs significantly from that of the U.S. The Mexican market is heavily weighted toward consumer staples, materials, industrials and financials, while technology stocks make up over one-third of the S&P 500's value. This divergence in sector composition further explains the low correlation.

Given these factors, Mexican stocks could be an intriguing diversification opportunity for U.S. market portfolios.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.description:

data.title: Occidental Petroleum: Berkshire's Deal For OxyChem Is A Win-Win
data.description:
I was planning to write about Occidental Petroleum (NYSE:OXY) (NEOE:OXY:CA) and the news that Warren Buffett’s Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) is buying up the company’s chemicals division for almost $10 billion is a win-win opportunity.

Clearing debt should secure Berkshire’s future support

My previous Buy recommendation on Occidental Petroleum was written in July 2022. That has underperformed the broader market with a total return of -14%, but including dividends, it is not too bad when the oil price is down by around 50%.

By reiterating the Buy recommendation, I see a chance to add further capital to the position in anticipation of an eventual turnaround for oil prices, alongside further dividends and buyback opportunities.

The opportunity is now even greater as the Buffett deal allows the company to pay down some of its long-term debt, narrow the company’s focus, and improve other areas of the balance sheet.

Those efforts will also mean that the company will likely be even more attractive for Buffett, who has been a big supporter of the oil firms in recent years.

Berkshire has a widely reported cash pile that was sitting at around $344.09B as of June 30, 2025. After completing the OxyChem deal, there is a lot of dry powder left for a well-loved oil company with a leaner balance sheet.

A closer look at the OxyChem benefits for Occidental

"This transaction strengthens our financial position and catalyzes a significant resource opportunity we’ve been building in our oil and gas business for the last decade," said Oxy President and CEO Vicki Hollub.

An investor presentation said the company will use the proceeds of the deal to repay around $6.5B of long-term debt.

Due to oil price deterioration, Occidental’s long-term net debt rose year-over-year from $18.39 billion to $23.34 billion at the end of Q2 2025.

The company will wind down around 25% of this number and clear up more than $350 million in annual interest expenses. This also has positive credit rating implications for any future borrowing.

The company outlined further benefits to shareholders in the presentation, saying the move achieves its target of less than $15 bn in debt, “accelerating shareholder returns.”

An “opportunistic, multi-year share repurchase program” would be possible, management added.

As I noted earlier, the company also said that more focus can be applied to oil and gas assets. This will include efforts to maximize current assets and create efficiencies. This has added benefits ahead of a possible rebound in oil & gas prices.

Second quarter earnings analysis

Annual revenue at Oxy surged from $17.8 billion at December 2020 to $36.63 billion two years later. The current trailing-twelve-month revenue figure is $27.15 billion.

However, the company is showing stability on a quarterly basis despite oil’s weakness. Cost of revenue remains at around $2.3-4 billion from March 2024. Net income is coming in with a floor at around $1 billion over the same period.

On valuation, the price/earnings ratio of Oxy has risen due to its price holding steady during oil's downturn and a sharp decrease in earnings per share.

This is a good sign as it means the company's share price has weathered what could be the worst of oil's decline. A leaner balance sheet, increased oil & gas focus and stronger commodity prices could supercharge the stock.

EPS peaked at $12.40 in 2022 and have since slipped to $1.69 in Q2 2025. An increase in oil & gas prices would have the effect of lowering the P/E ratio with a boost in EPS. That makes the P/E ratio a fair valuation of the opportunity at present, and future share buybacks will also boost returns.

Risks to the investment thesis

Some analysts were negative about the OxyChem deal, and that led to the shares falling -7.5% on Thursday. I believe that is boost for investors as we await better days in oil.

Analysts at Roth MKM said the deal could negatively affect free cash flow in coming years, after OxyChem was expected to contribute significant growth. I believe that my comments on valuation mean this is not an issue for investors. Scotiabank's Paul Cheng added that the price was a bit low after he had previously valued the division at $12 billion. I wouldn't be too worried about a $2 bn hit upfront, and the fact that Berkshire could be getting a sweet deal on OxyChem could pay dividends later.

The big risk to the investment thesis is obviously still in the oil price. The focus on artificial intelligence has added to a drought of capital moving into energy stocks, but that can change. Investors should remain focused on the conflict between Russia and Ukraine.

It has become clear in recent weeks that President Trump’s previous relationship with Vladimir Putin has soured. That leaves the door open to the risk of an escalation, and news on Thursday said that Trump risks a tough response from Putin by supplying Ukraine with intelligence on long-range energy infrastructure targets inside Russia.

Trump is weighing up the decision on whether to supply Ukraine with long-range missiles that could hit Moscow. The markets are ignoring this potential escalation and continue to grind higher.

Conclusion

Occidental Petroleum has had a slower few years after oil prices topped in May 2022. However, Warren Buffett has remained an avid fan of the oil firm, and his deal for the company’s chemical arm is a big win for Oxy. The company can make substantial improvements to its balance sheet and position itself for an improvement in oil prices. Buffett’s was a supporter of the firm ahead of this deal, and I would not be surprised if his next quarterly reports highlight further investment in Occidental.

data.title: OMAH: Extracting Dividends From Berkshire, 15% Distribution
data.description:
Thesis

Warren Buffett is a legendary investor, and Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.B) is a company that is admired by a huge number of investors. The name has produced stellar results throughout time by focusing on well-run businesses and holding them for prolonged periods. The only issue investors sometimes have with Berkshire is the lack of a dividend. The reasoning behind this tactic is that the company can use that capital more effectively than investors:

Buffett's investment prowess has delivered a 19.8% compounded annual return from 1965 to 2023, compared to the S&P 500's 10.2% compounded annual return over the same period. This is why he believes Berkshire shareholders are better served when he retains all the company's earnings and reinvests it rather than paying it out as dividends.

Capital markets however are able to layer structures on top of a portfolio in order to generate a dividend. This is the bread and butter of the equity buy-write funds as an example. VistaShares identified this opportunity in the market, and created the VistaShares Target 15 Berkshire Select Income ETF (NYSEARCA:OMAH) which in essence aims to extract dividends from the Berkshire Hathaway portfolio:

The VistaShares Target 15™ Berkshire Select Income Fund offers investors a core equity portfolio that generally mirrors the 20 largest holdings of Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B), as well as providing exposure to BRK.B directly, while leveraging the experience of the portfolio management team to seek to generate high monthly income.

The targeted distribution for this fund is 15%, hence the name of the ETF. In today's article we are going to have a close look at OMAH and its structure and benchmark it against an outright position in Berkshire.

Portfolio mirroring Berkshire

An investor can download the ETF's portfolio here, where they would get the following picture:

The fund contains 10.3% of outright Berkshire, while the rest is represented by the top holdings in BRK.B. Please note the rest of the holdings are covered calls written on each individual name. The calls are short dated (sub 1 month) and leave some upside in terms of price appreciation. Writing covered calls generates income, and leaving upside when structuring such calls ensures more of the upside is captured. Many investors would be very familiar with this structure from buy-write ETFs and CEFs which are now quite popular in the market.

If we look at the first option line in the file, namely 'AAPL US 10/17/25 C275' we can see this is a sold call on Apple (AAPL) with a October 17, 2025 maturity date and a 275 strike. AAPL is currently trading at $258 per share, so it is likely that the option will expire without being triggered. A short maturity profile ensures the fund takes advantage of the theta decay in the option.

Distribution mechanics

The fund is very up-front that it targets a 15% distribution, which is part of the nomenclature as well. As an investor, one should know by now that 15% does not come out of the blue sky, but an investment needs to generate that figure via cash flow or equity appreciation. Otherwise, an instrument such as an ETF disbursing 15%, will end up having a substantial NAV erosion over time. OMAH can rely on its income from writing covered calls and the NAV appreciation of the portfolio for its distributions.

The good news here is that Berkshire does have a very robust long term total return:

Morningstar presents the long term annualized returns, and we can see that figures are fairly close to that 15% figure. In essence, the fund will see its NAV accrete when the portfolio does better than 15% per annum, while the ETF will see its NAV shrink when it posts annual total returns below 15%.

When one looks at the Section 19a disclosures, we can notice the following: for September 2025, the return of capital figure is a high 93%, but one should not be worried. OMAH is not a fixed income fund, so thus it does not rely on actual cash flows for its distributions. In many instances we are going to see high ROC figures here, and we should not be worried. What matters is the fund's total return.

Analyzing the fund performance so far

The ETF is quite recent, having come to market in March 2025. Its total return since inception is as follows:

Long term, an investor should expect OMAH to mirror BRK.B or do better, given its mandate to build a similar portfolio and actively manage it via options. OMAH's sole purpose is to use derivatives to extract dividends, not to pick underlying equities that will outperform. Its portfolio will be a close proxy to Berkshire, and its active management might generate alpha via tactical trades in its covered call strategy. For example, the ETF might choose to stop writing calls during overextended moves to the downside, while it might also adjust tenors and upside during violent market rallies.

In its short life, the fund has performed very well, posting a positive total return for 2025 while Berkshire is down.

Some of the fund analytics so far are:

  • AUM: $0.58 bil
  • Expense ratio: 0.95%
  • Dividend frequency: Monthly
  • Distribution: 15%

Kindly note the following regarding this fund - if we end up in March 2026 with the fund also at the +5.3% level, then we would have a NAV move lower by roughly -10%. The 15% distribution leaves the NAV unchanged only in those years when the holdings are up 15% or more.

Berkshire is a conservative play

While the overall market keeps rallying and defying any valuation concept when it comes to P/E levels, Berkshire is a conservative play. As per Capital.com, the company holds $277 billion in cash, which is the largest in 30 years, and represents 22% of total assets.

OMAH does not hold 22% of its assets in cash, but does have a 10.3% allocation to Berkshire. In a downturn, one would expect OMAH to underperform Berkshire to a certain extent, although its portfolio of written calls might buffer some of that move. We saw an inkling of that during the April 2025 market move when OMAH was down much more when compared to Berkshire.

The 'cleanest' structure here would have been for OMAH to just hold BRK.B and write calls on that, but one would surmise the fund manager would have a harder time asking for 0.95% expenses for such a straightforward play.

Conclusion

OMAH is a new ETF that falls in the buy-write arena. The name aims to extract dividends from BRK.B and its portfolio via a covered call strategy. The ETF only has a 10% allocation to BRK.B, but its holdings mirror the Berkshire top holdings. The fund has a 15% distribution, but will see its NAV move lower during the years when the portfolio does not appreciate by that value. The ETF has outperformed BRK.B so far this year, and represents an attractive choice in a market where valuation levels are stretched.

data.title: SPS Commerce: Large TAM, Solid Moat, Renewed Sales Strategy
data.description:
Investment overview

I give a buy rating for SPS Commerce (NASDAQ:SPSC) as I believe its combination of an underpenetrated $11 billion TAM, a highly defensible network moat, and a powerful network-led growth motion will drive robust earnings growth ahead. With EPS set to nearly double over the next four years and the stock still trading at just ~23x NTM PE, I believe risk/reward skews firmly to the upside.

Business description

SPSC operates as a retail network, providing a comprehensive suite of cloud-based solutions that facilitate collaboration across the global retail supply chain. The main aim is to connect disparate trading partners like retailers, suppliers, third-party logistics providers (3PLs), distributors, and manufacturers to help them operate more efficiently. SPSC serves a broad range of clients, from retailers selling goods through physical and online stores to the suppliers that manufacture those goods and the logistics firms responsible for moving them. By streamlining communication and automating critical supply chain processes, SPS enables its customers to improve speed to market, drive sales and margin growth, and reduce operating costs.

Vast and Underpenetrated Market Provides Long Growth Runway

The scale of SPS's addressable market is what, in my view, anchors the long-term bull case. Per management estimates, the global TAM is $11.1 billion, with $6.5 billion sitting in the US alone. And of this large TAM, SPSC is only just ~7% (based on LTM revenue), which means the vast majority of the market remains untouched.

In other words, there is an enormous greenfield runway for SPSC to grow, particularly as it moves up the customer ladder. For context, SPS has historically done well with small- and medium-sized businesses, but adoption is not as great when it comes to larger enterprises. This is largely because large suppliers often rely on existing, deeply integrated systems they have built themselves. And hence, the opportunities to displace these systems are just not that many, and the common occasion for this opportunity to appear is when there are major change events like ERP upgrades. You see, system-wide overhauls are not common, and hence, the opportunity is low.

But the opportunity is huge and shouldn't be written off. In the segment of suppliers generating over $300 million in annual revenue, there are still thousands of potential customers SPS hasn't yet reached. The implications are huge if SPSC is able to gain traction here.

These accounts not only carry a higher average revenue opportunity but also bring more complex supply chain demands, which is where SPS's product suite adds the most value. Its fulfillment solution, for instance, streamlines core order data like POs and invoices, giving suppliers visibility across the chain. Assortment ensures item data (SKUs, images, etc.) are standardized for faster speed-to-market across channels. Analytics then help to provide demand forecasts and inventory insights from sell-through data. A large enterprise with multiple product lines, numerous retail partners, and high transaction volumes experiences all of these challenges simultaneously. Therefore, it derives significantly more value from adopting SPS's integrated suite.

Dominant Network

I believe SPSC's strongest asset is the sheer scale of its retail network, which is something competitors cannot simply replicate. This isn't just about having a large customer base; it's about how the ecosystem becomes more valuable with each additional participant (node).

Today, the SPSC network has more than 300,000 trading relationships, facilitating more than $650 billion in annual transaction value, managing 33 million unique SKUs, and processing more than 780 million documents. These are huge numbers, and the activity flowing through this network creates a proprietary stream of data on supply chain behavior, retailer requirements, and transaction patterns. For a new entrant to even compete on the same level as SPSC, it first has to get all this data, which is not available off the shelf. And importantly, this gap only widens as SPS continues to scale.

The flywheel effect is clear. Every new participant increases the network's utility to all members, which reinforces SPS's position and creates high switching costs. Here is how to envision this. Once a customer joins, the platform becomes deeply integrated into their essential business processes and systems. SPS handles the complexity of managing thousands of unique trading partner connections. Leaving would require a customer to rebuild this vast web of integrations, which is costly and operationally disruptive.

As such, there are very high switching costs. That advantage translates directly into operational leverage. For instance, SPS uses its network intelligence to pre-build trading partner connections, cutting onboarding timelines from weeks to days. The result is a faster time-to-value for customers and a stickier platform overall.

Network-Led Growth Motion to Unlock Next Wave of ARPU Expansion

While SPS's network has been its moat, the exciting part is how management is actively weaponizing it to fuel the next leg of growth. SPSC is leaning into what it calls a network-led growth strategy, which is a major shift from a largely reactive sales approach to a proactive one. The idea is straightforward. With all the data that SPSC possesses, it can easily identify expansion (cross/up-sell) opportunities that would otherwise take months or years for sales reps to uncover.

Perhaps a better way to explain this is by illustrating how it works. Take upselling, for example. The SPSC system can track a customer's document exchange volume, and when a sustained spike is detected, it automatically flags a qualified lead. That creates a timely opportunity for the sales team to move the customer into a higher-tier subscription. Cross-sell potential is even more compelling. By analyzing a supplier's transaction data, SPSC can highlight pain points like frequent chargebacks from a major retailer (say Walmart) and then generate an ROI case showing the savings from adopting SPS's Revenue Recovery solution. We should see substantially higher conversion rates since the sales rep would be using a customer's own data, which is far more persuasive than any generic sales script.

Even new logo acquisition benefits from this intelligence. The network can see when a supplier is engaging in retailer testing or certification but hasn't converted to a full SPS customer. Those high-intent prospects are then automatically funneled into CRM systems for targeted campaigns.

In my view, this shift is a major catalyst for both top- and bottom-line performance. It should accelerate ARPU growth by making upsell and cross-sell motions more timely and successful, while also boosting sales productivity by focusing reps on high-probability wins. As this ramps up, I believe it will start to report robust earnings growth, which should drive a positive re-rating of SPS valuation.

Valuation

Consensus estimates call for SPSC to deliver $6.52 adj. EPS in FY28, which implies almost a double from FY24 levels. This may seem like a lot, but I think it's possible given the large TAM (with plenty of growth opportunities) and the margin expansion opportunities from cross-selling. SPSC did more than double its adj. EPS from FY20 to FY24, so there is precedent. Besides, consensus has been fairly accurate on their estimates. In terms of multiples, SPSC trades at ~23x NTM PE as of the time of writing, which I think is too low considering its ability to double EPS in 4 years. Consider that during FY20, SPSC traded at ~50x NTM PE. Obviously, it is not wise to assume multiples expand back to 50x, but you can imagine the upside if that happens. Say it stays at 23x; multiplying that by $6.52, we get to a share price target of ~$150 vs. $103 today.

Risk

Near-term growth could soften as macro uncertainty prompts some customers to delay system upgrades that usually drive new deals. Comparisons are also tougher coming off the pandemic pull-forward, which may mute topline growth momentum. Looking further out, the risk of competition from well-capitalized peers will always be there. Imagine an irrational competitor with a strong balance sheet investing a lot of capital to compete. Even if they fail in the end, in the meantime, it would impact SPSC pricing power and the stock sentiment.

Conclusion

I give a buy rating for SPSC. It operates in a large TAM, has a dominant retail network, and its new go-to-market strategy all point to positive growth and ARPU expansion ahead. While risks around macro uncertainty and competitive intensity are worth watching, the long-duration growth drivers remain firmly intact. At today's multiple, I think the stock is cheap, and even without multiples expanding, there is still attractive upside.

data.title: Why Eli Lilly Is The Lion's Share Of My Portfolio
data.description:
Since my last Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) (NEOE:LLY:CA) analysis, the stock has gained 9% in price. I anticipate broader healthcare momentum to build in 2026. This is still an optimal entry point.

Healthcare as a sector is deeply underowned right now, setting up a very likely flow-driven re-rating cycle for the industry in 2026. LLY will be one of the biggest beneficiaries, given that it is the global leader in obesity therapeutics and very heavily weighted in industry indexes. The launch of Orforglipron is perhaps the most obvious sentiment inflection catalyst that is not fully priced into consensus modeling. Management is also deploying billions of dollars into supply-side capacity and diversifying the company's pipeline strategically into Alzheimer's, immunology, and oncology. This is one of the best pre-inflection investments on the market right now.

Orforglipron Sentiment Catalyst

At the moment, the truth is that GLP-1 needles carry a certain level of stigma, including storage and handling issues and generally lower compliance. The first oral attempt at a GLP-1 was Novo's (NVO) Rybelsus, and this showed efficacy but required an empty stomach, less than 4 ounces of water, and a 30-min wait as per the FDA label, which is a high daily burden. Lilly's Orforglipron simplifies dosing; it's taken once daily with no food or water restrictions. Orforglipron has already delivered ATTAIN-1 12.4% mean weight loss at 36 mg with cardiometabolic benefits, with tolerability similar to injectables. Evidently, this "true daily pill" is likely to lead to broader initiation, higher adherence, and a larger effective TAM.

Consensus already has a $100B obesity TAM by 2030, but the sell-side focus is still on injectable GLP-1 sales, and orals appear to be modeled conservatively. For example, Goldman Sachs seems to treat oral GLP-1s as a future balancing factor rather than definitive sentiment shifting catalyst. Goldman's downgrade of their anti-obesity market projection (from $130B to $95B global) describes price erosion, adoption, and coverage constraints as the central risk drivers, and it doesn't assume leapfrog substitution of orals. One can rationally assume that sentiment is more deflated right now than it ought to be due to conservatism from this consensus outlook, but also more broadly from healthcare's macro lag right now (more on that later). That said, I think the oral GLP-1s for Eli Lilly will build out the bull case for sentiment in 2026, among other factors.

We should remember that there's a competitive advantage here that will also likely dictate strong sentiment. As Orforglipron outperformed Novo's oral semaglutide in both A1C reduction and weight loss, the oral GLP-1 will end up as LLY's alone. What does this mean in the mind of the market? Potentially that the Novo vs. Lilly duopoly shifts to a Lilly platform monopoly, at least based on investor sentiment even if Novo retains a second-best lower-growth position.

In FY24 10-K, Lilly disclosed $548.1M of capitalized pre-launch inventory "primarily for Orforglipron," and that figure rose to $808.5M as of June 30, 2025. GAAP accounting standards mean that inventory can only be capitalized once regulatory approval is probable. Basically, you don't put $1B of unapproved drugs on your balance sheet unless you're sure the FDA greenlight is coming. This explains why investor sentiment for LLY stock is so stable right now (compared to the rest of healthcare, which has cratered temporarily).

Additionally, Lilly's manufacturing presence is now well-established with a heavy round of capex in FY2024, as Lilly announced a $9B expansion in Indiana, including for obesity drugs. In May 2025, Lilly committed $6.5B to build the world's largest API site in Houston, and it explicitly included Orforglipron and other small-molecule capacity. For those who can read between the very obvious lines, Lilly has already shown its hand: management expects Orforglipron to industrialize obesity care at a pill-based scale; you don't commit $6.5B to oral API infra if you believe otherwise.

Leadership & Governance Considerations

Under CEO Ricks, Lilly has repeatedly funded supply ahead of demand, which shrinks the supply-cap on revenue and keeps the multiple high by signaling visibility. Also, alpha is being engineered by management through diversification in Alzheimer's (Kisunla), immunology (Omvoh, now also Crohn's), and oncology (Jaypirca). And acquisitions like Loxo, DICE, Morphic, and POINT Biopharma show a willingness to buy strategic optionality, and perhaps more importantly, to "consolidate." Furthermore, the board's Science & Technology Committee gives clear pipeline oversight (strengthening long-duration investments), while the company's independent director model shows a governance structure focused on long-term capital compounding, given that incentives are heavily tied to the value of Lilly's stock.

Macro Context

When healthcare is underowned, even stable execution names with resilient growth benefit disproportionately from flow-driven re-ratings. Year-to-date, Health Care (XLV) has only risen a modest 1.18%, while the S&P 500 (SPX) has risen by nearly 14%. This shows an immense alpha opportunity if one has the patience to ride current market caution to the positive re-rating cycle. The current reset expectations due to the lower valuations mean that investors are exposed to easier revenue/EPS comparisons into 2026, and so positive surprises will be more common with reactions amplified.

LLY is XLV's largest weight at about 12%, and the sector's consensus leader on obesity therapeutics. This means that when flows come back to Health Care, benchmarking alone will drive demand toward LLY. Moreover, the flow of funds will be magnified by the oral GLP-1 sentiment cycle.

The most obvious macro upside catalyst for healthcare in 2026 is rate cuts, as in a falling rate environment, long-duration growth assets like LLY re-rate higher because discount rates fall. Also, policy clarity will remove the current discount. IRA-negotiated drug prices will go live, and so uncertainty will convert into confidence in the base case.

Given these dynamics, I maintain my current outlook that LLY will breach $1,200 in FY26 as the flow of funds re-rates the forward P/E multiple back toward ~35 with Fiscal 2027 normalized EPS of ~$35 in a very conservative base case. The current stock price is $830, so the implied upside is certainly over 40%.

Other Segments Beyond GLP-1s

Right now, much of the market sees Lilly almost entirely through the GLP-1 lens, but in reality, the company is not a one-drug story. Alzheimer's, immunology, and oncology represent the next steep growth curves that will keep Lilly's multiple elevated beyond obesity. This helps to cement LLY stock as a long-term compounder rather than a short-term thematic and momentum trade. That said, it appears likely that the stock's valuation will respond in cycles to the natural rhythms of demand in new fields, especially as growth from obesity drugs plateaus in standard S-curve fashion.

Kisunla is already FDA-approved for early Alzheimer's, treating mild impairment and mild dementia, placing Lilly among the very few with an on-market disease-modifying therapy. Uptake should also improve as sites standardize safety monitoring for ARIA (a known side effect), expand infusion capacity, and as payers and doctors gain comfort with new blood- and imaging-based diagnostic tools. If the operational barriers keep falling along the current trajectory, then Lilly's neuroscience business could become a true second growth engine alongside GLP-1 therapies.

Immunology exposure gives Lilly secure cash flows that reduce single-asset risk. While this might not be as high-growth as the neuroscience or obesity divisions, it's a steady and less cyclical contributor that can help to sustain Lilly's valuation multiples when GLP-1 sentiment wanes as growth moderates in 2-3 years. Specifically at this time, Omvoh was approved for ulcerative colitis in 2023, and for Crohn's disease in 2025.

Precision oncology also compounds value when you consider Lilly's foothold with Jaypirca, which is a next-generation BTK inhibitor that holds FDA approvals for mantle cell lymphoma and certain lymphocytic leukemia patients. Lilly also bought POINT Biopharma, which brought in a radioligand therapy platform, delivering targeted radiation directly to tumors. Oncology could, in fact, be Lilly's third growth engine long-term.

Risks

The most obvious headline risk is that if regulators impose stricter monitoring, delay approvals, or request more data, then uptake could slow for Orforglipron and pipeline assets in Alzheimer's, oncology, and immunology. This would push out revenue recognition and undermine near-term sentiment, but I consider this a low risk outcome given the current positive status of Lilly's medication pipeline and portfolio. Other obvious headline risks including side effects (like GI issues for Orforglipron), could lead to slower adoption amid periods of customer hesitation.

It's worth recognizing that any of these obvious headline negatives could compound poorly on the company's bottom line. For example, $15B+ in capex into new sites like Indiana, Houston API, and NC Concord, could end up with vacancy if sales come in under current expectations. Moreover, these new facilities must deliver flawlessly in their own right, or delays and shortages could disrupt scaling. So, there is a dual top-line and bottom-line risk here that is based on execution and customer reaction respectively.

Macroeconomically and politically, there is the chance for negative reactions on LLY. Inflows may not return on schedule if rate cuts are delayed or tech leadership persists so aggressively; the upside is really tied to sector rotation timing. Moreover, IRA drug-pricing reform may not look positive for LLY in future rounds which could drag on sentiment in later years; there is bipartisan concern over costs for high-value, high-demand drugs like those offered by Lilly. Then, there is the fact that political cycles are amplifying risk perception (the approaching midterms).

Conclusion: Strong Buy

Eli Lilly stock is currently opportunistically priced for remarkable 2026 alpha. The company is already well in the momentum of a generational obesity treatment revenue surge, and Orforglipron's differentiated oral profile combined with the company's robust supply chain infrastructure is likely to provide strong investor sentiment benefits in 2026 and beyond. Alzheimer's, immunology, and oncology pipelines also extend the growth runway beyond GLP-1s, while macro tailwinds like sector rotation, rate cuts, and policy clarity will logically strengthen the bull case. I do not think risks are severe enough to detract from my overarching opinion that LLY remains a Strong Buy.

data.title: Micron Is A Steal At All-Time Highs
data.description:
As you can see in the chart below, my ratings on Micron Technology, Inc. (NASDAQ:MU) have been quite accurate since late last year. Near the end of June, I reiterated my buy rating on the stock. I cited a strong data center business and an attractive valuation as key reasons for my bullish stance. Since then, the stock has surged another 46% to all-time highs.

In the below analysis, I find that the stock presents an attractive risk/reward proposition. The company's Q4 results were robust, and while Q1 guidance may be a bit mixed, the long term direction is very encouraging. Their technological leadership in HBM and their expanding capacity mean that growth will be strong in the coming years. Currently, the forward P/E ratio is at severely depressed levels, signaling that investors are unexcited about Micron's prospects. I believe that creates a massive opportunity, and thus I have decided to upgrade Micron to a strong buy rating.

Growth Inflection Point

Micron reported their FY2025 Q4 earnings just over a week ago and there are signs that top line growth has bottomed out. In the quarter, the company generated revenues of $11.32 billion, up 46% YoY. As you can see above, this is a new record for revenues, and they stated that this is "driven by AI data center growth." As for the growth rate itself, an inflection point seems to be in as revenue increases accelerated from the previous quarter's growth rate. Furthermore, the company was able to beat expectations by a rather nice margin of $159.82 million, and so overall Q4 was clearly a positive quarter.

When breaking down their revenues by business unit, the clear leader was Cloud Memory. This unit includes their all-important HBM business, and revenues surged 214% YoY. That is simply outrageous growth, and note that HBM revenues hit a fresh record. This indicates that demand for AI memory is currently explosive despite some investors' worries of a slowdown in infrastructure buildout. We'll talk more about the company-wide margin a little later, but their Cloud Memory segment saw impressive margin expansion. In addition to strong top line growth, profitability surged as gross margin was up 10 percentage points YoY while operating margin was up a whopping 15%. From these metrics, I believe it is clear that their most important business is firing on all cylinders currently.

HBM

Before moving on with the financial results of the company, let's discuss the direction of their HBM business. As the AI revolution has continued, HBM has become increasingly important to data centers but also to Micron as well. The company reported that their data center business represented 56% of revenues in FY2025, a new record. They also stated that their "HBM share is on track to grow again," and that is definitely what investors want to hear. While HBM3E is ramping rather impressively, attention is turning to HBM4.

Micron stated that their HBM4 "[delivers] industry-leading performance as well as best-in-class power efficiency." Performance and efficiency are the two things that customers look for and so I would say that Micron is very well positioned to see strong adoption of their cutting edge technology moving forward. Further out in the time line is HBM4E but Micron's strategic planning already seems encouraging. The company seeks to provide both standard and customized versions of HBM4E. With data center needs being different among different companies, it makes sense for Micron to work with these customers to make the most appropriate chip. This is likely to strengthen relationships, and in addition, Micron stated that "customized base logic die [will] deliver higher gross margins than standard HBM4E." Therefore, as the world shifts away from HBM3E, Micron may see even stronger financial returns.

In addition to technological leadership, Micron is well positioned to expand the capacity of HBM. The company has continued to invest in HBM assembly and testing and they stated that it would help them to meet demand in calendar 2026. They also reported good progress on the construction of their Singapore site. Supply from that facility is set to begin in calendar 2027. From these developments, it seems that Micron is aggressively pursuing the AI data center market and with supply of HBM likely to increase meaningfully over the next few years, investors should view the long term outlook as very favorable.

Profitability Soars

As stated earlier, margins for their Cloud Memory business expanded significantly YoY. For the company as a whole, profitability also made a major turn for the better. In adjusted terms, gross margin expanded 9.2 percentage points as an indication of strong pricing power and great cost control. Furthermore, great operational efficiency also boosted profitability. In total, operating expenses rose just 12%, and that led to the operating margin expanding by a whopping 12.5 percentage points. Overall, these are major improvements and show that strong top line growth is not coming at the expense of the bottom line.

For Q4, Micron reported an adjusted EPS of $3.03. That represents a 157% increase YoY. As you can see in the chart above, that is actually a deceleration from the previous quarter, but you really can't complain. This EPS figure beat expectations by a healthy amount as well as they surpassed estimates by $0.17. Moving forward, there may be some slowdown of bottom line growth, according to analysts, but you can see above that the growth rate is likely to remain at more than healthy levels.

Q1 Outlook

Micron provided Q1 guidance as a part of their earnings release. At the midpoint, they are expecting revenues to be $12.5 billion. That implies a YoY growth rate of 44%. That is a mild deceleration from Q4's 46% increase, and that may be a little bit disappointing. However, with HBM's long term outlook being strong, as discussed earlier, one quarter of deceleration is not enough to shake my confidence in the company. For gross margin, they are expecting 51.5%, and that is set to be another steep expansion YoY from 39.5%. Furthermore, operating expenses are only expected to increase by 28%, and so the profitability improvements that we have seen are set to continue. Lastly, for EPS, their $3.75 midpoint represents a 109% increase YoY. That is a significant deceleration from Q4 but nonetheless, a bottom line growth rate north of 100% shouldn't really be complained about. As a whole, I would say this guidance may be a bit mixed but near term robustness is still expected, and the long term outlook is unaffected.

Valuation Is Dirt Cheap

Currently, Micron's forward P/E is at a laughably low level. The multiple hit a major peak back in mid 2024 but at a current reading of 11.22, I believe the stock is dirt cheap. Firstly, note that the IT sector has a median non-GAAP forward P/E of 25.56 as of this writing. Therefore, Micron is at a massive discount. Let's sum up what we have learned about the company today.

Q4 results were strong both on the top and bottom lines as their data center business is thriving. Moving forward, the company is well positioned to be a key provider of HBM4 both due to technological advantages and due to expanding capacity. Further out in the future, the company already has a strategic plan on how they will want to ramp their HBM4E and so the company is on a great trajectory.

With Micron providing one of the key pieces of the AI infrastructure puzzle, I find this valuation to be puzzling. Investors seem to be unexcited about the company's future, and that is a mistake in my view. To conclude, I would say that Micron is severely undervalued currently, and that makes the risk/reward highly attractive.

Takeaway

In the market today, if you're a high-growth company that's involved with AI infrastructure, odds are that your valuation is quite high relative to the IT sector. Micron is offering great growth and a strong outlook at bargain prices. As stated just above, Q4 results were impressive, and the long term outlook for HBM makes the company's prospects brighter than ever. With that being the case, it is a major surprise that the valuation has remained subdued and is at massive discount. While the stock may be at all-time highs, the fundamental setup shows that there's a massive runway for more gains ahead, and as a result, I have decided to upgrade Micron to a strong buy rating.

data.title: Ambac: Surprise Acquisition Changes Outlook (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Ambac (NYSE:AMBC) is a specialty P&C insurer that I've covered many times. While I've rated it a Buy, a sudden acquisition that followed the long-awaited sale of their legacy guarantee business has changed the outlook somewhat. Consequently, I am downgrading this rating to Hold.

Recap of Previous Thesis

When I talked about AMBC last time, I went over how the company was in the midst of a multi-year turnaround effort. This stems from its pre-2008 days as a guarantee insurance company and efforts to reform into specialty P&C insurance since then.

While earnings showed seasonal trends, not always going straight up, the business was continuing to expand its book of business, across an increasingly diverse portfolio of policies. The main thing weighing the stock down over the past year had been the slow movement by the State of Wisconsin, as AMBC needed approval for the sale of their legacy guarantee business to Howard Marks's Oaktree Capital (OAK.PR.A)/(OAK.PR.B).

Based on the balance sheet at the time, I determined that book value per share would be $15.35. Additionally, the $420M deal would allow them to eliminate their $120M in debt and give them liquidity to navigate the next few years of their path to profitability. In anticipation of this deal, the hearing for which was early September, I reaffirmed my Buy rating.

Legacy Business Sold, Surprise Acquisition

After the market closed on September 29, AMBC announced that the sale of the legacy business had been completed. This was the outcome that investors, like myself, expected. Proceeds were used to pay down the outstanding debt related to the Beat acquisition the year prior. Moments later, however, they also announced the pending acquisition of ArmadaCare for $250M.

While the stock had been steadily climbing over $9 and toward $10, in anticipation of Wisconsin's final stamp of approval, the unexpected nature of this deal startled investors and brought the stock back down to lows closer to $8. The deal was underwritten by Truist (TFC), which included $120M in a new term loan and credit facility. This means they will only be debt-free until the deal closes, and this may partly explain the market's negative reaction.

These announcements were followed the next morning by a conference call that gave a business update.

According to management, the $250M acquisition price represents an adjusted EBITDA multiple of 13.8X. They expected contributions to earnings to be accretive as early as 2026.

They cited what made the deal attractive, the additional product diversification into accident & health and potential to grow the insurance book by a significant margin.

The long-term guidance for 2028 earnings goals did not change, still on the rough target of $80M in adjusted EBITDA.

CEO Claude LeBlanc stated:

With this transaction, we will be adding immediate growth and scale to our specialty MGA insurance distribution platform, consistent with our time line to achieve the $80 million to $90 million EBITDA aspirational goal, we previously set for 2028.

Outlook and Valuation

The changes in the outlook here aren't exactly huge, but there is a subtlety to how we treat the risk-reward asymmetry going forward. The earnings potential seems to be the same and may even be a bit more, but the trade-off is a balance sheet that had no debt and hundreds of millions in cash, producing interest income while short-term rates remain somewhat high.

If we reference the Q2 balance sheet again, the straight deal (after eliminating debt) would have given about $316M in cash. Combined with their $292M in investments, it would have done a lot more to cover unearned premiums and loss reserves of about $574M. Then there still would have been reinsurance on balance sheet to mitigate potential claims as well.

The income statement, meanwhile, points to expenses in excess of income of about $20M per quarter. Holding onto the cash would have given them a sufficient runway to meet their 2028 goals. While a larger business could earn more, any setback at this point would put the business in a much more precarious situation. The reward may be a bit higher, but the risk is as well.

Let's consider this question posed by the lone analyst in the call, Maxwell Fritscher:

And then finally for me, what's the expected magnitude of the data and technology investments? And what kind of time line do you anticipate those investments being on?

Normally, I would not quote and name an analyst in a thesis, but Fritscher was from Truist Securities. As a reminder, Truist is the underwriter of the ArmadaCare deal. CFO David Trick answered:

We do not have any immediate plans for additional M&A. That being said, if an -- some opportunity did arise for something that was attractive that would add diversity and growth to the book, it would -- we certainly would consider it, but it's not something that is a top priority for us at this time.

The focus is on the existing operations and driving organic growth of the existing operations and creating synergies as well as reducing costs across the platform, particularly at the holding company level. And as noted on the call, we will be reinitiating our stock buyback program, and those are our key priorities at this time.

I personally did not find this reassuring. The acquisition of ArmadaCare was not an immediate plan; selling the old guarantee business was. There were already a cost reductions, scaling, and buybacks on the table.

Moreover, the story from management over the past year has been expanding their distributor business, their non-risk-bearing business. This acquisition is a step away from that.

I also find it strange that the adjusted EBITDA outlook for 2028 doesn't change with this. $250M is no small deal for them. AMBC's own market cap is about $386M. I would think their medium-term guidance would at least reflect that. If the $250M is a multiple of 13.8X, then shouldn't the range increase to $90M to $100M, at the very least?

What do we, as investors, do about it? Well, I don't think the numerical values are sharply different until Q3 is reported, and even then it might not be much. There likely remains a large discount to book value, but the range of possibilities did get more negative, and I think that can be adjusted with a bigger margin of safety.

For me, AMBC does not become a Buy again until the stock goes below $7.

Conclusion

A business in turnaround would ideally prioritize risk management over growth. With cash in the bank and debt retired, Ambac would have been doing that as it scaled out its distributor business. The acquisition of ArmadaCare is a deviation from that. It may work out over time, but it's a weaker balance sheet that will not handle setbacks nearly as well.

As investors, we can manage our risk accordingly with a lower entry price. Since the stock still trades above that, I downgrade my rating to Hold until Q3 results and discussion provide better clarity on the positives of this deal.

data.title: Global Medical REIT's Turnaround: Leveraging Macro Tailwinds To Overcome Debt Worries
data.description:
Introduction

When I first covered Global Medical REIT (NYSE:GMRE) back in June, I titled the article “Dividend Cut, CEO Changed, And Likely Undervalued” to highlight the main ideas behind the investment, and although the stock saw a ~15% recovery initially, that was short-lived, being back almost exactly to the same price now.

The dividend cut was very likely priced in given the market’s nearly absent reaction when it actually happened, while the CEO change combined with other internal and external developments makes it probably even more attractive now, so let’s take another look and update the thesis.

Recent Quarters

Several things have happened with GMRE since I first covered them. First of all, their Q2 report was actually solid, with the AFFO per share finally increasing by 1 cent on the back of a significant ~9.5% increase in rental revenue.

As I said in the analysis, they cut the dividend by ~29% back in May and replaced the CEO later in June, in an effort to address their upcoming debt maturities and get a fresh, strategic perspective to guide their portfolio management and growth initiatives, as they say. The debt maturities are mostly represented by their drawn credit facility, which has basically always been the case for this company.

However, the market’s worry probably stems from the fact that Term Loan A ($350 million) matures in May 2026, Term Loan B ($150 million) in February 2028, and the Revolver ($202.6 million) in August 2026 - so most of it is in less than a year. However, these aren’t the company’s first maturities, and we’ve been in this situation before. The most likely outcome will be extending these for another half a decade or something, although they might come with a slightly higher interest rate given the current market environment. They even commented on this in the Q2 Earnings Q&A and were confident in how that will play out, as I’d expect them to be in their position:

So I mean, obviously, we have relatively more debt for a public company and a lot less debt than our private peers would have. I mean we're -- we sleep like babies. We've got great cash flows. We will stretch out our maturity ladder and that will feel better. 4x debt if it expires tomorrow is worse than 9x debt if your weighted average maturity is 7 years from now, we're not at either of those extremes. But we do have a large maturity obviously coming, and we're working on that, and we have all the confidence that, that will be achievable in the near term.

I believe that once they announce a positive resolution to this and extend these maybe in the 30s, a lot of pressure will be lifted from the stock, so that’s another impending catalyst that’s very likely to come soon (it’s not really a choice in this case, it has to come). Sure, even if it can result in higher interest and lower the AFFO, that can be counterbalanced by their potential development thanks to macro improvements such as rate cuts (will lower their interest as well) combined with the potential stock price appreciation that can allow them to issue stock to build accretive growth per share.

Also, back in August, they announced a one-for-five reverse stock split completed in late September, as well as an up to $50 million share buyback program to lower the volatility and improve its liquidity. This is a ~10.8% buyback yield, although the company is not obligated to exercise it and there’s no time frame, it’s just something that allows them to do it when they see fit, if they want to.

GMRE also reaffirmed its full year 2025 AFFO per share and unit guidance of $0.89 to $0.93. Note that this is based on the previous ~66.88 million shares outstanding, before the split which will lead to about 13.37 million shares. That’s an AFFO of about $60.2 million on the lower-end of the guidance, which is not bad at all, although it doesn’t include other acquisitions/dispositions, equity/debt issuing or one-time obligations related to the CEO succession plan. Still, $60 million on a ~$466 million market cap is definitely not something to ignore, and it’s what allows them to pay a massive yield even after it was cut.

Macro Developments

Of course, the main near-term catalyst that I cited in the previous analysis are rate cuts, which is a common catalyst for nearly every company out there. Following the September cut, the Fed is now expected to do 2 more cuts this year despite Powell saying he sees “meaningful” inflation ahead due especially to Trump’s tariffs bringing uncertainty, weakening the dollar and inflating prices for US consumers.

In GMRE’s case, as I said above, rate cuts can be very significant, as even a one percentage point interest reduction can mean significant accretion for the AFFO. Since their credit facilities are based on the SOFR rate, we’re already looking at a nice drop in September, with more coming as the Fed cuts the rates. As a note, their credit facility’s interest amounts to SOFR plus a related spread adjustment of 10 basis points and a borrowing spread based on the current pricing grid.

A recovery in that combined with directly reducing the interest and the debt of their tenants (who really need it, as the industry has been affected a lot by it lately), while allowing them to explore getting more leverage if needed to expand, especially after they’ll refinance the existing debt.

On top of that, this advanced another closely related catalyst: lower treasury yields. As rates are cut, treasury yields also fall (similar to SOFR), meaning that the theoretical risk-free rate will drop, resulting in the market having to adjust their valuations for the new risk-adjusted results since the risk-free rate drops down. This can result in more demand, especially for dividend stocks and REITs in particular, as their relative safety, low leverage, and high yields. This is also great for the preferred GMRE shares (NYSE:GMRE.PR.A), potentially leading to more demand for it as well and making it trade closer to the 7.5% yield.

Since GMRE’s AFFO could organically increase without the company doing anything (ignoring the refinancing), we’re looking at potentially higher dividends in the future once the company gets back on track and they feel confident in their financials again. Still, even now, an ~8.9% yield after the cut is really not bad at all, and the stock will also likely continue to move the opposite of interest rates given their balance sheet and business model.

Updated Valuation

To update the valuation, I’ll start again with $60 million in AFFO (slightly under their reaffirmed 2025 guidance), projecting 4% CAGR for the first 5 years thanks to rate cuts lowering their interest expenses and allowing them to expand while improving their tenants, 3% CAGR for the following 5, with a 2% terminal growth rate. I’ll use a 7.5% discount rate (above my estimated ~6.8%; assumptions: ~11.5% cost of equity, ~4% cost of debt) for a better margin of safety, with ~$709.6 million in net debt and ~13.37 million shares outstanding following the reverse split. This results in an Enterprise Value of $1.26 billion, and after accounting for the net debt, the Equity Value is ~$0.55 billion, leading to an intrinsic value per share of $40.98. Note however that rate cuts will be extremely accretive for this valuation, as getting that WACC even to 7% results in a price of $50.57 instead, which is a massive difference.

Conclusions

Overall, GMRE remains a Buy now thanks to the impending tailwinds that can boost the market’s confidence significantly, while the business remains solid and pays a very attractive dividend yield even after the cut. Risk remains in case the industry’s weakness persists, or we stick with a higher-for-longer rate environment, or the economy deteriorates further, but the overall reward seems to be well worth it for me, with this stock being very close to a Strong Buy rating for me.

data.title: General Motors: Not The Time For A Premium
data.description:
Investment Thesis

General Motors (NYSE:GM) has enjoyed strong bullish momentum over the last twelve months, but more precisely since April of that year, when tariffs hit U.S. equities hard at first glance, but subsequently, with the impacts better digested, triggered a lower risk perception, and the stock rallied further.

Although the Detroit-based automaker has shown itself to be quite capable of mitigating the impact of tariffs by around 30% with capacity realignment measures, the company is still promising guidance well below what was initially expected for 2025, with analysts being forced to revise their spreadsheets downwards.

However, as a rule of thumb, downward revisions suggest price pressure (at least in the short to medium term), and surprisingly, what happened was the opposite. GM continued to expand multiples with a market apparently not accounting for the impacts on the company's bottom line, even after the partial mitigation suggested.

And now, I believe GM's bullish thesis tends to be primarily driven by evidence of EBIT-adjusted margins recovering towards double digits, with 3Q25 (scheduled in October 21) being the first litmus test for this.

As for stocks that trade at a premium (even if in GM's case it's a premium vs. its own historical average), arguably it's necessary to deliver or better yet, overdeliver. I don't believe this will be GM's pattern in 3Q25 and potentially in 2H25, which, in my view, contradicts the company trading at a premium valuation at this very moment.

Therefore, I think GM deserves a Hold rating for now, waiting on the sidelines for better indications of margins close to pre-tariff levels.

From Double-Digit Margins to Downward Revisions

In recent years, especially between 2020 and 2023, GM has been delivering low-double-digit operating margins (EBIT) in the North America region, which is the company's profit-generating core. In fact, CEO Mary Barra and the management team have repeatedly communicated the goal of maintaining double-digit margins in the region, while accelerating the launch of EVs (Chevy Blazer EV, Equinox EV, Cadillac Lyriq).

However, the latest 2Q25 results pointed to a timid EBIT-adjusted margin coming in at 6.1% in North America, well below the ~10% envisioned, largely pressured by tariff headwinds ($1.1 billion impact).

Overall, EBIT-adjusted margins came in at 6.4%—it's important to note that "adjusted" here equates to the GAAP measure of net income attributable to stockholders, and therefore may vary from traditional EBIT margin metrics reported on platforms such as Seeking Alpha itself.

That said, in a way, it is no longer automatic to assume margins "above 10%" in the current environment—even though GM has already demonstrated that such levels are plausible in more stable conditions. So, in my interpretation, the main driver in GM's bullish thesis tends to be much more about stabilization and the ability to restore or protect margins as the transition to EVs progresses.

One point of note here is when we put side by side the difference in the price at which GM shares are priced and its EBIT margin (ttm) over the last three years, we can see that the spread between the two is at the highest historical level in the series, which in theory should reflect high market optimism (potentially with GM stock taking off from operating fundamentals) not necessarily consistent with an EBIT-adjusted margin scenario under pressure.

This implies that, in the current environment, investors need to be aware that a significant part of GM's positive momentum reflects future expectations, but that, curiously, these expectations have been sharply revised downward, which in most cases would have dampened the bullish momentum.

For example, when we look at analysts' expectations for 3Q25 and 4Q25, in the last six months, EPS of $2.87 and $2.37 were anticipated, while now $2.34 and $1.89 are anticipated—which means a reduction in expectations of 22.5% and 25.4%, respectively.

Even though the recent figures reported in Q2 2025 showed resilience in adjusted EBIT margins above expectations, given the severity of the impact of tariffs, the downward revision of EPS to less than 1% in 2025 and 2.6% in 2026 over the last three months is still modest compared to the previous outlook for GM's bottom line for 2025 posted in 4Q24.

Looking Ahead to Q3

It seems to me that the market is very optimistic that adjusted EBIT margins could return to levels of 8-10%, mainly due to the guidance provided by the CFO, indicating that in H2/25 EBIT will be ~$1.75 billion lower than in H1.

This is also because tariffs account for the vast majority of the decline in margins, much more than demand or pricing issues, with the market remaining solid (SAAR ~16.6 million), prices stable, and GM gaining market share.

However, Q3 is expected to be weaker than Q2 because (1) it will be another quarter full of tariffs; (2) lower wholesale seasonality; and (3) higher spending on new generation trucks and capacity expansion in the U.S.

Therefore, EBIT-adjusted margins will likely remain in the 6-7% range until tariff mitigation measures begin to take effect (new investments, 30% offset cited by CEO Barra, and cost discipline).

Another key secondary point, which I believe will be a topic of discussion on earnings day, is the focus on capital allocation, EV profitability, and the adoption curve.

Over the past three years, GM has reduced its outstanding shares by 4%, 17%, and 13.8%, respectively, spending a total of $20.6 billion between 2022 and 2024 on buybacks—$7.73 billion in the last twelve months.

In 2Q25, the management team reiterated open market repurchases resumed in July with $4.3 billion of remaining authorization, showing a commitment to returning capital when margins and cash flow allow—although in 2Q25 no shares were repurchased for the first time in several quarters.

GM's CFO emphasized that capital should be allocated in the coming quarters where it can structurally improve cost and performance. In other words, I anticipate that buybacks will take a prolonged pause while strategically prioritizing measures to enhance margins, while ramping up EVs in a sustainable manner.

Already making a link with EVs, I believe it will be crucial to monitor EV unit margins. In the case of the Chevrolet Equinox EV, in 2Q25, the vehicle gained ~6 points of market share in the segment (ICE+EV) year-over-year, showing strong market acceptance. GM already ranks Chevrolet as #2 in EVs in the U.S. and Cadillac as #5 overall.

In the case of Super Cruise, GM reported an expectation of $200 million from the vehicle in 2025 and doubling in 2026, with $4 billion in deferred software revenue. Advances in this line in 3Q25 towards these goals tend to be a very welcome sign.

Last but not least, in 2Q25, inventory and EV pricing adjustments (in this case, $600 million in write-downs) were temporary and should improve as production and demand stabilize. And that is precisely what needs to be seen in 3Q25, not the opposite.

On Valuations and Final Remarks

When we look at GM's forward EV/EBITDA and forward P/E multiples, it is quite clear that since 2025, there has been a clear re-rating on the stock multiples, as it has since traded at levels above its historical average over the last five years. In terms of domestic comparison, we can see that Ford Motors (F) has followed a similar trajectory.

As I see it, this re-rating (which took a brief pause in April due to tariffs) reflects a combination of successful strategies converging on GM delivering $14.9 billion in EBIT-adj. in 2024, close to the high-end of the guidance ($15 billion). And even though initially for 2025 an EBIT-adj. of $13.7-$15.7 billion was estimated (later readjusted due to tariffs to $10-12.5 billion).

But as GM reiterates its guidance (albeit lower than at the beginning of the year) in 2Q25, and as tariffs headwinds are likely to diminish, the thesis is proving to be more de-risked, paving the way for continued bullish sentiment and a seemingly new pattern of multiple established for U.S. legacy car makers, rather than any irrational valuation increase.

From my perspective, what’s happening with GM is that the market sees the tariff headwinds as less bad than expected, which is driving multiples higher—but it’s still a bad situation, and it’s going to keep the company from getting back to the double-digit EBIT-adjusted margins it worked hard to achieve over the past few years.

And because the company is at a stage of needing to "prove" to the markets that it can recover or stabilize operating margins, a premium valuation shouldn't make much sense at the moment, which leads me to believe that a correction is quite likely in the short to medium term.
